title.emphasis: Part IV: The Enforcement Conundrum: Breach, Remedies, and Reconciling the Standards

section.para: The theoretical and normative justifications for a bifurcated consent standard are compelling. However, the model's viability ultimately depends on its practical application in the context of a legal dispute. This final part confronts the enforcement conundrum directly, addressing the remedies available for breach, the central paradox of treating revocation of consent as a contractual violation, and proposing a novel interpretive framework that harmonizes the two consent standards without compromising the fundamental right to bodily autonomy.

title.emphasis: A. The Unavailability of Specific Performance

section.para: The first and most straightforward issue of enforcement concerns the remedy of specific performance. In contract law, specific performance is an equitable remedy where a court orders a breaching party to perform their contractual obligations.44 This remedy is typically reserved for situations where monetary damages are inadequate, such as in contracts for the sale of unique goods or real estate.3

It is black-letter law in California, as in all American jurisdictions, that courts will not grant specific performance for personal service contracts.47 This long-standing rule is rooted in two powerful justifications. First, ordering an individual to perform a personal service against their will would constitute involuntary servitude, a violation of the Thirteenth Amendment to the U.S. Constitution. Second, there are profound practical difficulties in judicial supervision of such a remedy; a court cannot effectively monitor the quality and willingness of a compelled performance.47

This rule applies with absolute and undeniable force to any contract involving sexual acts. No court in California would ever issue an order compelling an individual to have sex. The very idea is antithetical to the foundational principles of law and liberty. This absolute bar on specific performance is legally significant because it reinforces a core tenet of the bifurcated model: the physical performance of a sexual act can never be legally mandated by a contract. The only possible legal basis for the performance of such an act is the contemporaneous, voluntary, and affirmative consent of the individual. The contract can set the stage, but it cannot compel the performance.

This leads directly to the central conflict: if specific performance is unavailable, can the non-breaching party sue for monetary damages when the other party revokes sexual consent? If Party A signs a contract to engage in a series of sexual encounters with Party B for a fee, and then revokes consent during the first encounter, can Party B sue Party A for the unpaid fee for that encounter, or for consequential damages representing the lost benefit of the entire bargain?.3

To allow such a lawsuit would be to create a legal framework that is both incoherent and dangerous. It would place the contractual interest in receiving the "benefit of the bargain" in direct and irreconcilable opposition to the public policy of protecting sexual autonomy. The threat of being sued for financial damages would create immense pressure on a person to continue with an unwanted sexual act. This economic coercion would operate as a powerful "chilling effect" on the exercise of the right to revoke consent. A person might feel compelled to endure an unwanted sexual experience to avoid a costly lawsuit, effectively negating the "free and voluntary" aspect of the affirmative consent standard.

The law cannot simultaneously grant a person the absolute right to say "stop" while also allowing them to be financially penalized for exercising that right. Such a system would render the right of revocation hollow and illusory. As established by the analogy to medical informed consent, where a patient's revocation does not give rise to a breach of contract claim by the hospital for its economic losses, the law must prioritize the right of bodily autonomy over the economic expectations of a contract. Therefore, the act of revoking sexual consent cannot be legally cognizable as a breach of contract giving rise to a claim for damages.

title.emphasis: C. A Proposed Interpretive Framework: Consent as a Condition Precedent

section.para: To resolve this paradox in a way that is consistent with both contract law principles and public policy, this report proposes that California courts adopt a specific rule of construction for any enforceable agreement involving sexual performance. This rule would treat the ongoing, affirmative consent of the performing party not as a contractual duty or a promise, but as a condition precedent to the other party's duty to pay and to the performing party's own duty to perform.

In contract law, a condition precedent is an event or action that must take place before a party is obligated to perform their contractual duties. If the condition does not occur, the duty to perform is excused, and the failure to perform is not a breach of contract. Applying this framework to a sexual agreement would work as follows:

listitem.para: Contract Formation: The agreement itself would be formed under the standard objective "meeting of the minds" test. This initial contract would validly bind the parties to its general, non-sexual terms, such as confidentiality, scheduling logistics, and the rate of payment for any encounters that do occur.

The Condition Precedent: The obligation for Party A to engage in a sexual act at any given moment, and the corresponding obligation for Party B to pay for that specific act, would be subject to a crucial condition precedent: the existence of Party A's affirmative, conscious, voluntary, and ongoing consent at that moment.
Failure of the Condition: If, at any point, Party A does not provide or actively revokes their affirmative consent, the condition precedent is not met.

Legal Consequence: Because the condition fails, the contractual duty for Party A to perform the sexual act never arises for that moment. Consequently, Party A's refusal to perform is not a breach of contract. It is simply the non-fulfillment of a condition, which legally excuses the performance.

section.para: This interpretive framework elegantly resolves the enforcement conundrum. It allows the general terms of the contract to remain enforceable. For example, if a consensual encounter did take place, Party B would be in breach if they failed to pay the agreed-upon fee. Likewise, either party could be in breach for violating a confidentiality clause. However, the framework makes the act of revoking sexual consent legally inert from a breach of contract perspective. It is not a breach; it is the exercise of a fundamental, legally protected right that prevents a contractual duty from ever materializing. This approach allows the two distinct consent standards to coexist and operate in their proper spheres. Contract law governs the bargain, while the principles of affirmative consent govern the body.
